highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.121 Released
highlights.summary: The latest release adds an `alwaysLoad` option to MCP server config so specified tools are always available without waiting for tool-search deferral — great for frequently used servers. It also introduces `claude plugin prune` to clean up orphaned plugin dependencies, keeping your environment tidy. Use `plugin uninstall --prune` to cascade-remove unused dependencies in one step.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.119 Released
highlights.summary: Settings configured via `/config` (such as theme, editor mode, and verbose output) now persist automatically to `~/.claude/settings.json` and respect the full project/local/policy override chain. A new `prUrlTemplate` setting lets teams point the PR badge footer to a custom code-review URL instead of the default GitHub link. This makes Claude Code much more portable across different team setups and CI environments.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.118 Released
highlights.summary: This release adds vim visual mode (`v`) and visual-line mode (`V`) to Claude Code's editor, making keyboard-driven text selection and editing much faster for vim users. The `/cost` and `/stats` commands have been merged into a single `/usage` command with tabbed views — both old shortcuts still work. You can now also create and switch between custom named themes directly from the `/theme` command.

highlights.title: An Update on Recent Claude Code Quality Reports (April 23 Postmortem)
highlights.summary: Anthropic published a transparent postmortem addressing community reports of degraded Claude Code output quality, explaining what went wrong and the steps taken to fix it. If you noticed Claude Code behaving unexpectedly in recent weeks, this post explains the root cause and confirms a resolution. It's a good read for understanding how Anthropic monitors and responds to model quality regressions.

highlights.title: Introducing Claude Opus 4.7
highlights.summary: Anthropic has announced Claude Opus 4.7, the latest and most capable model in the Opus family, which can be used directly within Claude Code for complex agentic tasks. Developers using Claude Code can switch to Opus 4.7 via the `/model` command to leverage its improved reasoning on difficult coding challenges. This is a significant upgrade for anyone tackling large refactors, multi-file tasks, or intricate debugging sessions.

highlights.title: How to Use Claude Code in VS Code (Bilibili: 如何在VS Code中使用Claude Code)
highlights.summary: This Bilibili video walks through the complete process of setting up and using Claude Code inside VS Code, making it ideal for developers who prefer a GUI over the terminal. The tutorial covers installation, connecting Claude Code to your workspace, and running your first AI-assisted coding tasks. It's a practical starting point for Chinese-speaking developers or anyone who learns best by watching a step-by-step walkthrough.

highlights.title: Claude Code Desktop App: Beginner's Guide (No Terminal Needed)
highlights.summary: This YouTube tutorial shows how to get started with Claude Code's desktop app without ever opening a terminal, which lowers the barrier significantly for developers new to CLI tools. It covers the core UI, how to open a project, and how to issue your first prompts to get coding assistance. If the command line feels intimidating, this is the perfect entry point into the Claude Code ecosystem.

highlights.title: Ask HN: How Are You Using AI Code Assistants on Large, Messy Legacy Codebases?
highlights.summary: This Hacker News thread gathers real-world strategies from experienced developers on applying AI coding tools like Claude to large, complex legacy projects where context is limited and code quality is inconsistent. Readers share prompting techniques, scoping strategies, and honest assessments of where AI assistance falls short. If you're trying to introduce Claude Code at a company with existing technical debt, the discussion offers grounded, practical advice.

tips_of_the_day: Use `alwaysLoad: true` in your MCP server config for tools you reach for constantly (like a database schema server or internal API browser) — it eliminates the tool-search delay and keeps them instantly available every session.
Run `claude plugin prune` periodically to remove orphaned auto-installed plugin dependencies; a leaner plugin environment means faster startup times and fewer unexpected conflicts between tools.

If you're switching to Claude Opus 4.7 for a tough task, type `/model` inside Claude Code to change models on the fly without restarting — your session context is preserved, so you can escalate to a more powerful model mid-conversation when a problem turns out harder than expected.
